title: Everyday Slang
description: Sixteen casual German expressions young native speakers actually use - the slang that makes you sound natural, not like a textbook.
situation: Hanging out with friends, texting, and everyday casual chat where formal German would sound stiff.

phrases.tip: The classic German one-word greeting. A reply of 'Na?' back means 'good, you?' - the whole exchange can be just two 'Na's.
Hugely common for 'great', e.g. 'Das ist voll geil'. Literally it can mean something cruder, but in everyday speech it just means 'awesome'.
Works for both good and bad shock - 'Das ist ja krass!' is 'whoa, that's insane'. The go-to reaction word for under-30s.
Friendly address term among (mostly male) friends. Literally 'old one'. Also used alone as an exclamation: 'Alter!' = 'no way / oh man'.
'Ich hab Bock' = 'I'm keen', 'keinen Bock' = 'can't be bothered'. Literally 'to have goat'. Everyday and very common.
'Wir hängen einfach ab' = 'we're just hanging out'. The casual word for doing nothing in particular with friends.
Borrowed from English and fully German now. 'Lass uns chillen' = 'let's chill'. Also 'chillig' as an adjective for a laid-back vibe.
Northern (very Hamburg/Berlin) friendly address term, like 'Alter'. Used a lot in youth speech and rap; only with friends, never with strangers or elders.
Literally 'in no case'. A firm 'absolutely not'. Widely acceptable in any setting, formal or casual.
Playful 'null Bock' variant meaning 'I have no desire to do that'. Very casual, only with friends.
Literally 'that's sausage to me'. A classic, slightly playful way to say you don't care either way.
'Red keinen Quatsch' = 'don't talk nonsense'. Also a warm dismissal: 'Quatsch!' = 'oh come on, don't be silly'. Mildly cheeky, never rude.
Literally 'I'm totally flat'. Used for both exhaustion and being amazed/speechless, depending on tone.
Short for 'lass es mal'. A breezy way to wave something off. 'mal' is the all-purpose German filler softener sprinkled everywhere.
Literally 'off it goes'. The standard rallying cry to get moving or start something. Fine in any setting.
Intensifier filler used by younger speakers, e.g. 'Das ist voll cool' = 'that's really cool'. Literally 'full'. Sprinkle it before adjectives.
